name: Rashied Ali
abstract: Rashied Ali, born Robert Patterson (July 1, 1933 – August 12, 2009), was an American free jazz and avant-garde drummer who was best known for performing with John Coltrane in the last years of Coltrane's life.

interviews.title: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 30, 2009

concerts.label: McCoy Tyner Trio with the New Art Ensemble and the Pharoah Sanders Sextet
Clifford Thornton New Art Ensemble, with Jayne Cortez, Poet, and the Rashied Ali Quintet

quotes.text: then even some of the main jazz clubs will hire like the really name avant garde players like Cecil Taylor or Ornette Coleman and sometimes I even get a shot at it too, because I’m sort of like an old-timer now. And so we get to play in a lot of the major clubs here now. But in the 70s it wasn’t about to be had, you know. We were hurting for places to play, we were hurting for recording companies to record us, so we were forced to start our own kind of venues. We went into like into loft situations in New York City and people started fixing up lofts and putting on their own concerts and putting on their own things, they called it “loft jazz” or “loft times” and then we started putting out our own records, actually the whole deal. Like recording them, pressing them up, and selling them on our own or either taking them to distributors and getting a deal with the distributors to put them out and to get them around to record stores and stuff like that. You know. So actually we were like self-made in the 70s because we wasn’t getting any help from the major companies or the major clubs you know. But we survived. We survived. We got people to come out to listen to us, we sold our records, and then I mean we did so well we even had our own jazz festival called the New York Jazz Musicians’ Festival which was a festival that sort of bumped heads with George Wein’s Newport Festival, because no avant garde festival was on that. And we sort of put a dent in their thing. We got a lot of people that was coming from Europe, and they was coming to hear us more than going down to Carnegie Hall and listening to, you know, what George Wein had. And so George Wein made a deal with us actually to make us a part of his festival, which was unheard of. And so we did that, I mean that’s a long story. I didn’t get involved in it because I felt like a lot of things that wasn’t being done that should have been done, but some of us went and George Wein got them a place and advertised it on his Newport stuff and got avant garde people a venue, a couple of venues, for them to play on his festival as well. And then a lot of the clubs started giving us jobs, because we had our own places anyway, to play, so they just got in on the act. And so actually man, we were kind of self-made. We sort of forced them to recognize who we were and what we were doing, and giving us employment and giving us gigs and giving us record dates.
quotes.caption: Mentions Carnegie Hall in context of Newport Festival competition
quotes.doc_title: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 30, 2009
